title: Early Redundant Tracking For Family Reunification
stem: During a mass casualty incident (MCI) drill, the EMS branch can account for ambulance destinations, but the reunification branch cannot match three pediatric patients because one hospital used temporary aliases, one crew documented only triage color, and one family arrived before the transport log was reconciled. The public information officer asks whether reunification should simply wait until all hospitals finish registration. What preparedness change most directly addresses the failure?

options.A: Have each hospital use its own registration alias process and report aggregate destination counts to EMS, while EMS limits its role to transport destination assignment.
options.B: Post a controlled family inquiry number with aggregate destination counts and hospital contact procedures, without reconciling field identifiers to hospital receipt.
options.C: Build redundant patient-tracking links from triage through transport and hospital receipt, with a controlled reunification process shared by EMS, hospitals, public safety, and state partners.
options.D: Assign a transport-unit liaison to call families from destination logs after transfer of care, using crew judgment to decide which pediatric cases need reunification support.

rationale: C is best because the failure was loss of identity continuity across triage, transport, hospital receipt, and reunification. A improves hospital-side registration but leaves the cross-system link broken. B creates a family-facing contact mechanism but does not reconcile field identifiers to hospital receipt. D adds a liaison function but remains crew-dependent and incomplete for a multiagency pediatric reunification problem.
citation: Millin MG, Jenkins JL, Kirsch TD. Mass casualty management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:312.

title: Vehicle Capability Versus Ambulance Count
stem: A fire-based EMS system is choosing between adding one ambulance or adding two medical first-response sport-utility vehicles with automated external defibrillators, hemorrhage-control equipment, and supervisor response capability. Finance wants to compare only patient transports per vehicle-hour. What analysis is most useful for medical oversight?

options.A: Compare billable transports, unit-hour cost, response-time effects, and whether nontransport vehicles measurably improve time to first clinical intervention.
options.B: Compare mission, staffing model, response role, clinical equipment, lifecycle cost, and how transport and nontransport assets change time to useful care.
options.C: Compare ambulance availability, supervisor availability, vehicle utilization, and time to first defibrillator or hemorrhage-control capability before choosing.
options.D: Compare lower purchase price, faster first response, geographic coverage, and the remaining transport-capacity gap if supervisor vehicles are selected.

rationale: B is best because the source frames vehicle choice around mission, staffing, cost, and response function, including nontransport medical first response. A includes useful operational measures but reduces the question to productivity and first intervention, missing mission fit, staffing, equipment, and transport-capacity tradeoffs. C is closer but incomplete because it omits cost structure and broader mission requirements. D considers real deployment advantages but underweights the residual transport-capacity gap.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:5.

title: Just Culture Versus Disciplinary Contamination
stem: A medication-safety committee reviews near misses, but attendance collapses after two reports are forwarded directly into disciplinary files without a separate behavioral review. A recent narcotic-dosing error was caught before administration, but the crew did not report it. Which redesign best matches a just-culture quality system?

options.A: Route all quality reports first to discipline so staff understand that near misses are as serious as adverse events.
options.B: Separate learning review from behavior/accountability review, protect human-error reporting, and feed lessons back so crews see real-time hazards corrected.
options.C: Accept only reports with patient harm because non-harm events are too subjective for quality-improvement work.
options.D: Let supervisors decide privately which near misses deserve committee review, then withhold feedback until annual training.

rationale: B is best because just culture supports shared, nonpunitive hazard identification while preserving a separate path for reckless behavior or accountability concerns. A recreates the chilling effect. C loses near-miss learning. D suppresses transparency and timely feedback.

title: Regional Resource Request Spillover
stem: After a plant explosion, County A asks the regional coordination center to send every available burn bed and critical-care ambulance to its incident. County B simultaneously reports smoke-exposure patients from a related highway crash and has no local critical-care transport. Which regional coordination action is most defensible?

options.A: Allocate scarce regional specialty resources using a shared view of both counties’ needs, specialty capability, transport assets, and downstream effects before committing all assets to the first request.
options.B: Fill County A’s request first because the incident that generated the first regional request has priority over later requests.
options.C: Route each request to hospital bed managers and let individual facilities negotiate burn transfers, transport priority, and resource release times directly with local dispatch.
options.D: Split regional resources evenly between counties by population and incident size, even if burn capacity, transport acuity, and receiving capability are unequal.

rationale: A is best because the problem is regional allocation under simultaneous demand, requiring common situational awareness and downstream impact analysis. B uses arrival order rather than regional need and capability. C gives hospitals a role but abdicates medical coordination of scarce cross-jurisdictional assets. D sounds equitable but ignores unequal acuity and specialty capacity.
citation: Rimstad R, Braut GS. Literature review on medical incident command. Prehosp Disaster Med. 2015;30(2):205-215.

title: Diversion Policy With Regional Harm Signal
stem: A hospital requests automatic diversion whenever its emergency department waiting room exceeds 25 patients. Six months of regional data show diversion hours rose, ambulance turnaround did not improve, and two neighboring hospitals had longer offload delays during diversion periods. What is the best medical-director response?

options.A: Honor diversion whenever the hospital reaches the waiting-room threshold and require real-time ambulance-turnaround reporting to validate crowding and patient-flow pressure.
options.B: Keep the threshold but require monthly reporting of diversion hours, wall times, and left-without-being-seen rates because transparency alone resolves regional effects.
options.C: Reevaluate whether diversion should remain available using regional flow, ambulance-turnaround, destination-delay, and patient-safety data rather than treating the hospital threshold as controlling.
options.D: Rotate diversion permission among hospitals by week and publish equal-use targets so every hospital receives relief regardless of patient-flow data.

rationale: C is best because diversion is a regional system intervention and should be judged by patient-flow and safety effects across the system. A uses a single hospital threshold and misses regional harm. B adds useful reporting but assumes measurement alone fixes the policy. D is procedurally equal but clinically arbitrary.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:39.

topic: Physician directed care by radio or phone
title: Online Orders Within A Written Exception Pathway
stem: A severe asthma protocol allows magnesium sulfate only after online physician approval. During a 30-minute transport, the crew also asks the online physician to authorize an unlisted ketamine infusion if magnesium fails. The medication is stocked for another indication, but there is no credentialed airway-sedation pathway for asthma. What order is most appropriate?

options.A: Authorize magnesium if clinically indicated under the written exception pathway, but do not use the radio order to create an unlisted ketamine-infusion pathway outside scope and credentialing.
options.B: Authorize both medications after documenting physician name, time, and patient condition, because direct orders can bridge protocol gaps during a life-threatening call.
options.C: Refuse magnesium and ketamine because online medical control should not modify standing orders or exception pathways once transport has begun.
options.D: Delegate approval to the operations supervisor because stocked medication availability, pump setup, and transport timing make this mainly a logistics decision.

rationale: A is best because online medical control may operate within a written exception pathway, but it should not create a new uncredentialed medication pathway by radio order. B is tempting during a life-threatening call but overstates direct medical-order authority. C ignores the existing written exception pathway. D confuses medication scope and clinical authorization with logistics.

title: Task-Based Personal Protective Equipment
stem: During one shift, crews perform four tasks: suctioning copious respiratory secretions from a coughing patient, assisting childbirth with heavy fluid exposure, starting an IV on a dry forearm, and cleaning a stretcher after vomiting. Which personal protective equipment plan best follows standard precautions?

options.A: Use gloves for patient contact, add eye protection for childbirth, use a respirator during stretcher cleaning, and reserve gowns for visibly soiled clothing.
options.B: Use gloves and eye protection for the IV start, add a gown for stretcher cleaning, use a respirator for childbirth, and use a surgical mask alone for suctioning.
options.C: Use gloves during patient care, add face protection after splash is seen, and add a respirator when screening suggests airborne infection.
options.D: Gloves for all tasks; add face and eye protection for suctioning; add gown plus face and eye protection for childbirth; match cleaning protection to splash and soilage risk.

rationale: D is best because personal protective equipment is task-specific and should match anticipated exposure to blood, body fluids, splash, soilage, and respiratory risk. A underprotects suctioning and misplaces respirator use. B mismatches respirator, gown, and face-protection choices to the likely exposures. C waits until splash is seen and underprotects tasks where splash or soilage is foreseeable.
citation: MacDonald RD. Infectious and communicable diseases. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:210.

topic: Graphic display tools (run chart and control chart)
title: Control Chart Versus Run Chart After Intervention
stem: A team tracks monthly scene-to-electrocardiogram time after a feedback intervention. The run chart shows eight points below the prior median, and the control chart shows one point outside the lower control limit. The team wants to declare the intervention permanently effective. What is the best interpretation?

options.A: Treat the control-limit crossing as evidence of nonrandom change, stop the initial pilot, and spread the intervention without further monitoring.
options.B: The pattern suggests special-cause variation worth investigating, but the team should confirm process change and sustainability before declaring permanent success.
options.C: Use the run-chart median shift as the sole decision rule because control limits are mainly for manufacturing and add little to clinical improvement work.
options.D: Return to a fishbone analysis and abandon time-series review until staff can identify every cause of delay before and after the intervention.

rationale: B is best because control charts can signal special-cause variation, but the signal requires investigation and continued monitoring. A is tempting because the signal is real, but it overclaims causation and sustainability. C underuses control limits. D confuses causal-analysis tools with performance-over-time displays and would stall learning from the data already collected.
citation: Bourn SS, Mackey KE, Redlener M. Defining, measuring, and improving quality. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:440.

title: Determinants That Matter Only If Response Changes
stem: A dispatch committee is shortening caller interrogation. One proposed change removes age/sex and near-syncope questions from abdominal-pain calls because “abdominal pain usually gets the same ambulance anyway.” In this system, those findings trigger an advanced life support cold response rather than basic life support. What is the best medical-director position?

options.A: Remove the questions and rely on crew assessment after arrival, because determinant details mainly support documentation and quality review.
options.B: Remove the questions from dispatch but allow responding crews to upgrade after arrival if they identify pregnancy-related risk.
options.C: Keep the questions only for callers who volunteer pregnancy, syncope, or severe bleeding before the dispatcher starts chief-complaint interrogation.
options.D: Keep clinically discriminating questions when the answers change response level or mode, and monitor undertriage and overtriage after any protocol change.

rationale: D is best because dispatch determinant questions should be retained when they change response priority, mode, or instructions, and the effect should be monitored after protocol change. A and B remove potentially discriminating information. C waits for unsolicited caller information rather than using structured interrogation.

title: NIMS Component Behind The Information Failure
stem: During a flood response, EMS, hospitals, shelters, public works, and emergency management each maintain separate bed counts, road closures, and evacuation totals. Resource requests are duplicated because agencies cannot see the same operational picture. Which National Incident Management System (NIMS) component is most directly failing?

options.A: Resource management only, because shared typing, inventorying, and status tracking of ambulances solve most information problems.
options.B: Command and coordination only, because incident command structure should replace separate agency information processes during response.
options.C: Public information only, because the main operational issue is whether residents, media, and elected officials hear one public message.
options.D: Communications and information management, because decision makers need standardized information processes that support a common operating picture.

rationale: D is best because the described failure is fragmented information and lack of a common operating picture. A addresses resource status but not the broader information-management process. B confuses command structure with shared data. C addresses outward messaging rather than responder decision support.

title: Finger Thoracostomy Program Boundaries
stem: A critical care transport service wants to add finger thoracostomy for selected ventilated trauma patients with suspected tension pneumothorax during prolonged transport. The medical director is deciding how to structure the procedure. Which approach is most defensible?

options.A: Limit it to a credentialed critical-care pathway with indications, equipment, pleural-entry confirmation, rescue planning, and quality review.
options.B: Teach it to all emergency medical technician crews for chest trauma after a didactic module and annual skills attestation.
options.C: Allow paramedics to use it after any failed needle decompression but track only survival to hospital arrival.
options.D: Reserve it for physician response vehicles and exclude transport clinicians even when they have critical-care training.

rationale: A is best because finger thoracostomy is a specialized procedure that needs training, indications, confirmation, rescue planning, and review. B makes it too broad. C lacks adequate governance and measures only a crude outcome. D is narrower than necessary for trained critical-care systems.

title: Psychosis De-Escalation With Safety Boundary
stem: EMS is called for a man who says neighbors implanted a transmitter in his walls. He is pacing and frightened but unarmed, follows simple directions, and becomes louder whenever responders challenge the belief. A police officer asks whether EMS wants him restrained immediately. What is the best first approach?

options.A: Ask police to restrain immediately because any fixed delusion makes verbal engagement unsafe.
options.B: Explain repeatedly that the transmitter is impossible, because correcting the delusion is necessary before transport refusal can be assessed.
options.C: Use calm, simple de-escalation without arguing about the belief, reduce stimulation, and keep a withdrawal plan if weapons or extreme agitation emerge.
options.D: Leave unless he requests transport, because discussing a delusional belief is inherently coercive.

rationale: C is best because supportive de-escalation is preferred when the patient is not armed or extremely agitated, and scene safety boundaries still matter. A is premature. B is likely to escalate. D abandons a patient who may still need assessment and transport planning.
citation: Reich JH, Ng AT. Behavioral health emergencies. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:477.

title: Insulin Pump And Route-Specific Hypoglycemia Care
stem: A 42-year-old with type 1 diabetes is confused, diaphoretic, and unable to swallow safely. The glucose is 38 mg/dL. An insulin pump is running under the patient’s shirt, family reports vomiting and poor intake, and IV access has failed once. Which treatment plan best avoids both anchoring and undertreatment?

options.A: Review pump history and basal/bolus settings first, start vascular access during transport, and wait to give dextrose until pump malfunction versus dehydration is clarified.
options.B: Suspend or remove the pump according to protocol, treat hypoglycemia by the safest available route while continuing vascular-access attempts, and monitor for recurrence or another cause of altered mental status.
options.C: Give oral glucose after positioning the patient, then reassess pump settings and vomiting history during transport if mental status improves enough to protect the airway.
options.D: Leave the pump running and prioritize IV fluids for vomiting and suspected dehydration, because the glucose may normalize once perfusion improves.

rationale: B is best because the immediate priority is safe hypoglycemia treatment while stopping ongoing insulin delivery and continuing reassessment. A delays treatment for device history. C uses an unsafe route for a patient with altered mental status and vomiting. D focuses on dehydration while continuing the likely contributor to hypoglycemia.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:189.

topic: Application of backboard as extrication device
title: Active Extrication Boundary For Spinal Motion Devices
stem: A frail older adult with possible cervical spine injury has been moved from a stairwell to the ambulance using a rigid board. He is now on the ambulance cot, normotensive but tachypneic from chest trauma, and transport will take 55 minutes. A crew member asks whether the board should stay in place because the patient has possible spine injury. What is the best medical-director guidance under the current National Association of EMS Physicians (NAEMSP) evidence review?

options.A: Use the board as an active-extrication tool, then transition to cot-based spinal motion restriction that preserves alignment while reducing respiratory and pressure-injury harms.
options.B: Keep the patient on the rigid board for the full transport because post-injury movement is the best-supported cause of delayed neurologic deterioration.
options.C: Move the patient from the board to a full-body vacuum splint for the entire transport because vacuum devices eliminate pressure and pulmonary-function harms.
options.D: Remove all spinal precautions after transfer to the cot because there is no definitive evidence that any motion restriction strategy improves outcomes.

rationale: A is best because the National Association of EMS Physicians (NAEMSP) review supports limiting backboards and full-body vacuum splints to active extrication while balancing possible motion concerns against documented harms. B overstates movement as the dominant supported mechanism and prolongs board harm. C ignores that vacuum splints may be more comfortable but do not eliminate pressure or pulmonary-function concerns. D overcorrects by abandoning spinal motion restriction rather than using a less harmful strategy.
citation: Millin MG, Innes JC, King GD, et al. Prehospital Trauma Compendium: Prehospital Management of Spinal Cord Injuries - A NAEMSP Comprehensive Review and Analysis of the Literature. Prehosp Emerg Care. Published online August 7, 2025. doi:10.1080/10903127.2025.2541258.

title: Due Process Thresholds After Practice Restriction
stem: An EMS agency reviews three clinician actions after separate protocol concerns: a written warning with no pay or rank change, transfer to a different station with no pay change, and a 5-day suspension from clinical practice with a report to the state certification office. Administration says none require procedural review because no license has been permanently revoked. Which statement best fits the due-process discussion?

options.A: Use an internal operations-review policy only, because permanent revocation is the sole action that triggers procedural due process.
options.B: A practice-limiting suspension or probation may require notice and a review process, while a warning or no-pay-change transfer may not create the same protected interest.
options.C: Apply the full revocation hearing process to every written warning, but allow short suspensions to remain informal if the policy limits them to under one week.
options.D: Use separate paid-employee and volunteer policies, because certification limits and volunteer clinical restrictions do not require a review pathway.

rationale: B is best because the source distinguishes minor employment actions from actions that limit a license/certification or practice interest. A overstates the rule by limiting due process to permanent revocation. C overstates warnings while understating suspensions. D incorrectly narrows due-process analysis and omits review for possible practice restrictions.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:171.

title: Physician Field Supervision Without Role Confusion
stem: An EMS physician is riding with a field supervisor for clinical supervision. On a complex dyspnea call, the crew is forming an assessment and treatment plan, the patient is stable enough for ongoing evaluation, and no immediate safety threat exists. The physician is tempted to take over the interview and procedures to demonstrate the preferred approach. What field-supervision behavior best matches the source?

options.A: Assume direct patient care whenever the physician is present, because responsibility for the case transfers from the crew to the medical director on arrival.
options.B: Avoid introducing the physician to the patient or other responders so the crew’s normal behavior is not altered during field observation.
options.C: Use field rides primarily to identify disciplinary issues, with teaching and corrective feedback deferred until a later chart-review meeting.
options.D: Introduce the physician role when feasible, let the crew carry out assessment and treatment, use Socratic questions or feedback only as needed, and step in mainly for patient-safety threats or rare difficult procedures.

rationale: D is best because field clinical supervision should support bedside teaching without unnecessarily disrupting crew-patient interaction; direct intervention is reserved for patient-safety concerns, added support, or unusually difficult procedures. A confuses supervision with routine takeover. B misses recommended introductions and team integration. C converts supervision into retrospective discipline rather than real-time education.

title: Likelihood Of Benefit In Scarce Antidote Allocation
stem: A chemical release produces 12 symptomatic patients eligible for a scarce antidote, but only six doses remain. Two patients are minimally symptomatic, four have severe exposure but signs suggesting reversibility, and six are pulseless or moribund despite initial care. Which preplanned allocation criterion is most defensible for individual antidote dosing?

options.A: Prioritize patients most likely to receive meaningful medical benefit from the antidote, with the goal of maximizing lives saved and outcomes across the affected population.
options.B: Give doses strictly in arrival order at the casualty collection point, using triage-tag sequence to avoid making value judgments under pressure.
options.C: Give doses to the sickest patients first when they have the most abnormal vital signs, even if the antidote is unlikely to change outcome.
options.D: Give doses to mildly symptomatic patients first when they require fewer staff resources, then reassess severe patients after transport capacity improves.

rationale: A is best because scarce antidote allocation should focus on likelihood of meaningful benefit and population outcome. B is administratively simple but not ethically optimized. C may spend the antidote where benefit is unlikely. D favors low-resource patients without regard to who most needs or benefits from the antidote.

title: Controlled Pressure For Post-Tonsillectomy Bleeding
stem: A 12-year-old is spitting brisk blood five days after tonsillectomy. He is sitting upright, anxious, and maintaining his airway, but bleeding continues despite suction. The crew has gauze, hemostats, and epinephrine available under protocol. Which local bleeding-control maneuver is safest while preparing for rapid transport and airway deterioration?

options.A: Place loose gauze into the oropharynx until bleeding slows, then leave it undisturbed for transport.
options.B: Position the patient supine and encourage swallowing so clot formation is not disrupted by suctioning.
options.C: Apply controlled pressure with retrievable gauze held by an instrument, using protocol-approved topical medication if allowed.
options.D: Limit care to suction and notification because local pressure should wait until surgical visualization.

rationale: C is best because direct pressure can help but gauze must remain controlled and retrievable to avoid airway obstruction. A risks lost packing and obstruction. B worsens aspiration risk. D ignores a temporizing measure during ongoing bleeding.

title: NGO Mass-Care Role Versus Medical Assets
stem: After a flood evacuation, EMS triage is complete. The shelter’s main needs are food, water, temporary shelter, transportation, volunteer coordination, interpreter support, disability-related assistance, and help identifying displaced survivors. A Medical Reserve Corps unit is separately supporting public health vaccination planning, and a federal medical team is supporting a damaged hospital. Which partner assignment best matches the disaster-management source?

options.A: Use organized non-governmental partners such as voluntary-agency networks or the American Red Cross for mass care, logistics, survivor-identification support, interpreter/disability assistance, and volunteer coordination while medical agencies handle medical escalation.
options.B: Assign the Medical Reserve Corps to all food, water, shelter, transportation, and family-identification functions because its members include health professionals.
options.C: Request a Disaster Medical Assistance Team to run routine shelter logistics because any shelter with evacuees should be treated as a federal field medical site.
options.D: Assign law enforcement to interpreter, disability-access, transportation, and family-identification support because those activities may generate investigative information.

B overextends the Medical Reserve Corps beyond its public-health volunteer role. C medicalizes mass-care logistics. D assigns support functions to law enforcement rather than organized disaster-support partners.

title: First-Trimester Bleeding With Shock
stem: A 10-week pregnant patient has heavy vaginal bleeding, severe cramping, pallor, and a blood pressure of 82/48 mm Hg. Her partner is distraught and asks whether the pregnancy can be saved. Which EMS action should be prioritized?

options.A: Provide reassurance and routine obstetric follow-up because miscarriage is common before 12 weeks.
options.B: Focus on collecting tissue for pathology and defer resuscitation until obstetric viability is clarified.
options.C: Ask the patient to ambulate to the ambulance to reduce scene time and avoid worsening bleeding by movement.
options.D: Treat hemorrhagic shock with resuscitation and rapid transport while providing emotional support and early receiving-facility notification.

options.C: The team needs to know this was a gastrojejunostomy-type device because gastric replacement alone may not restore jejunal feeding access.
options.D: EMS should advance a replacement tube past the pylorus when the caregiver knows the prior external length.

rationale: C is best because a gastrojejunostomy uses a gastric entry but extends into the jejunum, so simple gastric replacement may not restore postpyloric function. A and B ignore tube location. D describes an unsafe blind advancement approach.

title: Compression Pauses On Defibrillator Review
stem: A cardiac arrest review shows good compression depth and rate while compressions are occurring, but pauses of 18 to 30 seconds occur for airway attempts, pulse checks, and moving the patient to the cot. What change is most likely to improve resuscitation quality?

options.A: Reduce no-flow time by choreographing rhythm checks, airway attempts, device changes, and movement around continuous compressions.
options.B: Prioritize early intubation because airway completion is more important than compression interruptions.
options.C: Move the patient to the ambulance immediately so definitive care can begin even if compressions pause repeatedly.
options.D: Extend pulse checks until every rescuer agrees whether a pulse is present.

rationale: A is best because no-flow fraction worsens survival even when depth and rate are adequate during compressions. B, C, and D all accept avoidable pauses for lower-priority tasks.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:134.

title: ePCR Architecture And Workflow Burden
stem: A sepsis project fails because crews hand-enter monitor vitals into a free-text note after transport. The electronic patient-care-report system has no device import, no discrete field for first hypotension time, and no hospital-outcome exchange. Which design change best supports both workflow and measurement?

options.A: Add a required quality-review sepsis note that prompts crews to type first hypotension time, first lactate if obtained, and receiving-hospital disposition after each call.
options.B: Create a supervisor quality-abstraction queue that manually extracts first hypotension time, monitor values, and hospital outcomes from narrative notes and records.
options.C: Purchase a reporting system dashboard that displays sepsis compliance from current narrative fields while postponing monitor integration and outcome exchange.
options.D: Use workflow-timed discrete fields, direct monitor data integration where feasible, and exchange capability for hospital outcomes before relying on back-end reports.

rationale: D is best because usable information systems need front-end data capture, discrete data, device integration where feasible, exchange capability, and back-end reporting. A adds structure but still makes crews retype data and does not solve outcome linkage. B may recover some data but is labor-intensive and less reliable. C improves display without fixing the missing discrete inputs and linkage needed for valid measurement.
citation: Mears G. Data management and information systems. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:521.

topic: Airway management in challenging conditions (e.g., low light, atypical position)
title: Low-Light Airway Failure As Readiness Failure
stem: During a nighttime arrest in a poorly lit bedroom, the laryngoscope light is dim, the spare handle has dead batteries, and no backup light is in the airway kit. The intubator struggles despite otherwise adequate technique. Which system fix is most appropriate?

options.A: Add airway-kit checks for working illumination, spare power, protected storage, and backup techniques for low-light conditions.
options.B: Add an annual airway lecture and leave the equipment-check process unchanged because the main problem is technique.
options.C: Purchase video laryngoscopes but keep the same spare-battery, backup-light, and protective-storage process.
options.D: Plan to move patients to brighter locations before attempts whenever lighting is poor, even during active resuscitation.

rationale: A is best because low-light airway failures often reflect equipment-readiness problems and need checks, spares, storage, and backup techniques. B blames skill alone. C may add equipment but leaves readiness gaps. D creates unsafe delays in time-critical patients.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:44.

title: NDMS Patient Movement Through A Federal Coordinating Center
stem: After a hurricane, local and state officials identify ventilated patients who must be evacuated from an overwhelmed region to hospitals outside the disaster area. A receiving Veterans Affairs facility is prepared to function as a Federal Coordinating Center with a patient reception area, triage/staging, and tracking to participating hospitals. Which coordination path best matches the source?

options.A: Request a Disaster Mortuary Operational Response Team with a fatality-management coordination policy, because federally coordinated patient tracking after a disaster is primarily a morgue mission.
options.B: Use an Emergency Management Assistance Compact mutual-aid coordination policy, because interstate license recognition is the same mechanism as federal patient movement.
options.C: Assign a Disaster Medical Assistance Team field-clinic coordination plan at the sending hospital and keep ventilated patients in-region until local capacity returns.
options.D: Use the National Disaster Medical System (NDMS) patient-movement function, with local/state patient identification, federal coordination of transport, Federal Coordinating Center reception, and distribution to participating hospitals able to meet patient needs.

rationale: D is best because the source describes the National Disaster Medical System (NDMS) as providing patient movement to participating hospitals when local medical systems are overwhelmed, with local/state identification, federal transport coordination, Federal Coordinating Center reception, triage/staging, and tracking. A confuses patient movement with fatality management. B addresses mutual aid/licensure rather than federal patient evacuation. C may support field care but does not solve out-of-region critical-care evacuation.
citation: Catlett CL, Jenkins JL, Millin MG. Disaster preparedness and management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:289.

topic: Working with government and public health agencies
title: BLS Oversight When State Authority Is Silent
stem: A county wants its EMS medical director to impose a new basic life support (BLS) clinician credentialing and suspension process by county protocol. The state EMS agency has no statutory language addressing BLS medical oversight, although state law controls clinician licensure and scope. The same county also wants shorter response-time targets in its ambulance contract. Which governance answer is best?

options.A: The county protocol can create statewide BLS medical-oversight authority, scope limits, and licensure consequences because EMS operations are locally delivered.
options.B: Creating a BLS medical-oversight mandate or practice limitation likely requires state statutory or regulatory authority, while local response-time targets belong in county contracting with medical-director input on patient-care effects.
options.C: The National Registry should decide both the BLS practice-limitation process and the county response-time contract because national certification controls EMS operations.
options.D: Hospital medical-staff bylaws should control both issues because EMS clinicians deliver health care and transport patients to hospitals.

rationale: B is best because the source notes state-to-state variation in medical-direction requirements, that some states lack BLS statutory language, and that many medical-director functions may occur at local, county, regional, or state levels. A overstates local authority over licensure/scope. C confuses certification with state/local governance. D assigns EMS system governance to hospital bylaws.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:5.
generation_notes: Second-pass easy-item hardening: replaced generic state/local split with source-specific BLS statutory-silence and local contract boundary.

title: Alternate Care Facility Authority And Credentialing
stem: A county plans to open a gymnasium alternate care facility during a respiratory surge. The site has cots, oxygen, and volunteers, but the plan does not identify who can activate it, whether emergency declarations change liability/scope rules, how outside clinicians will be credentialed, or how EMS destination decisions will be coordinated regionally. What is the most important readiness correction?

options.A: Build a cache list for cots, oxygen, medications, food service, and infection-control supplies, then let the emergency operations center decide activation details during the event.
options.B: Have each hospital send overflow patients with its own clinicians and paperwork, then reconcile scope, liability, and credentialing rules after the first operational period.
options.C: Preidentify staffing rosters and clinical triage categories, but use emergency declarations to resolve outside-provider credentialing and EMS destination authority at activation.
options.D: Define legal activation authority, declaration-dependent protections, external-provider credentialing, clinical scope, communications, and regional destination coordination before activation.

rationale: D is best because alternate care facilities require prospective governance, legal authority, staffing/credentialing rules, communication, and regional coordination. A handles logistics but leaves activation and legal authority unresolved. B preserves hospital throughput but creates preventable scope and liability ambiguity. C addresses staffing and triage but still waits too long to settle credentialing and destination authority.
citation: Catlett CL, Jenkins JL, Millin MG. Disaster preparedness and management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:291.

title: MRC Integration Versus Walk-In Volunteers
stem: During a public-health shelter operation, a local Medical Reserve Corps (MRC) unit offers nurses, pharmacists, interpreters, and logistics volunteers. The unit coordinator says members were identified, screened, trained, and organized before the event and routinely work with the health department, emergency management, hospitals, and partner organizations. Several unaffiliated clinicians also arrive at the shelter door. Which integration plan best fits the source?

options.A: Treat MRC members and unaffiliated walk-in clinicians identically at the shelter door, because prior screening would reduce flexibility during surge operations.
options.B: Place the MRC under an independent federal command structure for all shelter operations because national funding makes local public-health coordination optional.
options.C: Limit MRC volunteers to ambulance transport tasks because shelter support, public-health activities, interpretation, and logistics are outside MRC disaster roles.
options.D: Use the MRC through its local unit coordinator and existing public-health/emergency-management partnerships, while routing unaffiliated clinicians through the planned credentialing process before clinical assignment.

rationale: D is best because the source describes MRC units as local groups of identified, screened, trained, and organized volunteers led by a local coordinator and working with health departments, emergency management agencies, hospitals, and partner organizations. A discards the value of an organized volunteer unit. B overfederalizes a locally integrated resource. C inappropriately narrows the MRC role.
citation: Catlett CL, Jenkins JL, Millin MG. Disaster preparedness and management. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:287.

topic: Mechanical CPR and real-time feedback devices
title: Mechanical CPR At A Staffed Arrest
stem: A witnessed ventricular fibrillation arrest occurs at a staffed airport clinic. Six trained rescuers are present, compressions are high quality with planned rotations, defibrillation is ready, and extrication is not needed. A mechanical compression device is available in the ambulance. What is the best initial approach?

options.A: Apply the mechanical device immediately because it is always superior to manual compressions.
options.B: Stop compressions for device placement before the first shock so later compressions are more consistent.
options.C: Continue high-quality manual compressions and rapid defibrillation; reserve the device for situations where manual quality or transport conditions justify it.
options.D: Avoid defibrillation until the device is placed because shocks are less effective during manual cardiopulmonary resuscitation.

rationale: C is best because mechanical cardiopulmonary resuscitation is not the initial priority when enough rescuers can provide excellent manual compressions. A and B overprioritize the device. D dangerously delays defibrillation.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:135.

title: Maintaining Nasotracheal Intubation In Scope
stem: An EMS system has kept nasotracheal intubation in protocol, but no clinician has performed it in three years and the agency has no simulation, competency, or adjunct training for the skill. The medical director is reviewing airway scope. Which decision is most defensible?

options.A: Keep the skill for every paramedic because rarely used airway procedures are best preserved by protocol language alone.
options.B: Either restrict it to clinicians with ongoing training and competency verification or remove it if the system cannot maintain the skill safely.
options.C: Make nasotracheal intubation the default difficult-airway plan because it avoids paralytics and is blind.
options.D: Keep it only for pediatric patients because low use in adults does not affect pediatric skill retention.

rationale: B is best because nasotracheal intubation is skill dependent and now uncommon in many EMS systems; retaining it requires training and competency. A relies on paper scope. C overstates its role. D invents a pediatric exception and ignores skill maintenance.
citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:32-33.
